blurb: An airy, subshrubby xeric mint that fizzes with a cloud of tiny pale lavender-to-white flowers from midsummer to frost over small aromatic leaves — one of the longest-blooming, most bee-beloved plants for hot, lean ground.
water: Low to moderate; drought-tolerant once established
seasons: Fine mounded aromatic foliage spring; haze of tiny lavender-white flowers midsummer to frost; dies back over winter
wildlife: Outstanding late-season bee and butterfly nectar plant; alive with pollinators in bloom
origin: Mediterranean S Europe, N Africa & SW Asia
habitat: Dry sunny banks, scrub & rocky ground

care.hardiness: Hardy roughly USDA zones 5a–7b, so reliable across the Front Range (Boulder ~5b–6a) in well-drained ground. It can be a short-lived perennial, but it carries on by gentle self-sowing where happy.[1][2]
care.planting: Plant in spring (after the mid-May last frost) or early fall from nursery transplants into a sunny, sharply drained spot; lean, gritty soil is ideal. In containers use a free-draining gritty mix — it makes a fine aromatic patio plant — but in a pot the crown is far less cold-hardy, so overwinter pots in a sheltered spot or unheated garage and keep them on the dry side in winter.[1][2]
care.sun: Full sun — six-plus hours — for a dense, free-flowering mound; it gets floppy and shy-blooming in shade.[1][2]
care.soil: Well-drained loamy, sandy, or rocky soil; tolerant of a wide pH from about 6.0 to 8.0, so Boulder's alkaline clay is fine as long as drainage is sharp. Wet, heavy winter soil is the main killer — amend clay with grit if needed.[1]
care.water: Low to moderate. Water through the first season to establish; afterward it is notably drought-tolerant and needs only occasional deep watering in summer heat. Avoid overwatering and standing winter moisture.[1][2]
care.spacing: Space about 18–24 in apart — plants form wide, airy mounds that knit together into a haze of bloom.[1]
care.propagation: Easiest from spring division of established clumps, or from softwood cuttings in early summer; it also comes from seed and self-sows modestly. Lift and divide every few years to keep the clump vigorous.[1]
care.bloom: A long season of tiny tubular white-to-lavender flowers from mid/late summer through frost — one of the latest and longest bloomers in the dry garden. Light shearing after the first flush can renew the display.[1][2]
care.feeding: Little to none — it flowers best in lean soil; rich feeding produces lax, floppy growth at the expense of the airy habit.[1]
care.maintenance: Cut the dead stems back to the basal mound in late fall or early spring. Shear lightly midseason if it sprawls; deadhead or shear after bloom to limit self-sowing if you want to contain it.[1][2]
care.selfsow: Self-sows modestly where conditions suit it — usually welcome, but shear spent flowers before seed set if you'd rather keep it in bounds.[1]
care.troubles: No serious pests or diseases; its aromatic foliage is generally pest-free and deer-resistant. Root and crown rot are the only real risk, in wet or poorly drained soil.[1][2]
care.harvest: Snip aromatic leaves anytime in the growing season for a fresh or dried minty-oregano tea or as a culinary seasoning; flavor is strongest just before bloom.[1]

edible.summary: An aromatic culinary mint — the leaves (and edible flowers) make a pleasant minty-oregano herbal tea and seasoning.[1]
edible.parts: The small aromatic leaves are the part used; the tiny flowers are also edible and can garnish or flavor dishes.[1]
edible.preparation: Use the leaves fresh or dried, steeped as a mint-lemon/oregano-scented herbal tea or chopped as a culinary seasoning much like a milder oregano/savory; harvest just before flowering for the strongest scent.[1]
edible.caution: A long-used culinary herb of the mint family with no notable toxicity, but as with any aromatic mint use it as a flavoring herb in ordinary culinary amounts, not as a bulk food. Be sure of your identification — confirm the strong minty-oregano aroma — before using any wild-foraged mint, and as always introduce a new herbal tea in moderation.[1]
